The prophet Isaiah declared judgment against Memphis: 'Noph shall have distresses daily' (Isaiah 19:13). Jeremiah and Ezekiel both prophesied its desolation (Jeremiah 46:14, Ezekiel 30:13). The city's decline from the world's greatest capital to ruins fulfilled these prophecies precisely.
cities.scholarly_context: Memphis (ancient Men-nefer, biblical Noph) was founded around 3100 BC as the capital of unified Egypt. The site near modern Mit Rahina contains the ruins of the great temple of Ptah, a colossal fallen statue of Ramesses II, and an alabaster sphinx. Sir Flinders Petrie excavated the Palace of Apries (1908-1913), and the Egypt Exploration Society conducted further work in the 1950s-1980s. Artifacts are in the Egyptian Museum, Cairo and the open-air museum at Mit Rahina.

cities.name: Thebes
cities.description: Thebes, known biblically as No-Amon ('city of Amon'), was the magnificent capital of Upper Egypt and the center of worship for the Egyptian god Amun. The Most High specifically declared judgment against Thebes, saying He would punish 'Amon of No' (Jeremiah 46:25). Ezekiel prophesied that the Most High would 'execute judgments upon No' and 'cut off the multitude of No' (Ezekiel 30:14-16). Nahum used the fall of Thebes to Assyria in 663 BC as a warning to Nineveh: 'Art thou better than No-Amon?' (Nahum 3:8).
cities.scholarly_context: Thebes encompasses the modern city of Luxor and the vast temple complexes of Karnak and Luxor on the east bank, with the Valley of the Kings and mortuary temples on the west bank. Auguste Mariette began systematic excavation of the temples in the 1850s, and Howard Carter discovered Tutankhamun's intact tomb (KV62) in 1922. The Merneptah Stele (c. 1208 BC), found at Thebes by Flinders Petrie in 1896, contains the earliest known extra-biblical reference to 'Israel.' It is housed in the Egyptian Museum, Cairo.

cities.name: Ur
cities.description: Ur of the Chaldees was the birthplace of Abraham and the starting point of the most consequential journey in biblical history. The Most High called Abram to leave Ur and go to a land He would show him (Genesis 15:7, Nehemiah 9:7). Abraham's father Terah died in Haran after departing from Ur (Genesis 11:28-32). Ur was a sophisticated city of moon-god worship, making Abraham's faith in the one true God all the more remarkable. Stephen recounted this calling in his speech before the Sanhedrin, noting that 'the God of glory appeared unto our father Abraham, when he was in Mesopotamia, before he dwelt in Haran' (Acts 7:2).
cities.scholarly_context: Sir Leonard Woolley excavated Ur (Tell el-Muqayyar) from 1922-1934 for the British Museum and the University of Pennsylvania. He uncovered the Great Ziggurat of Ur-Nammu, the Royal Cemetery with its spectacular gold treasures (including the Standard of Ur and the Ram in a Thicket), and extensive residential quarters. Woolley identified a deep flood deposit that he linked to the biblical Flood narrative. Artifacts are divided between the British Museum, the Penn Museum in Philadelphia, and the Iraq Museum, Baghdad.

cities.name: Haran
cities.description: Haran was a major crossroads city in upper Mesopotamia where Abraham's family settled after leaving Ur. Terah, Abraham's father, died there (Genesis 11:31-32), and it was from Haran that Abraham departed for Canaan at the age of seventy-five (Genesis 12:4-5). Jacob fled to Haran to escape Esau's wrath and lived there for twenty years, marrying Leah and Rachel (Genesis 27:43, Genesis 29:1-30). The Assyrians conquered Haran and boasted of destroying its gods (2 Kings 19:12). It was also the site of the last Assyrian capital after the fall of Nineveh in 612 BC.
cities.scholarly_context: Ancient Haran (modern Harran, Turkey) preserves ruins of the world's first known Islamic university and the remains of the great Temple of Sin (the moon god). D.S. Rice conducted excavations in the 1950s, uncovering Umayyad and Abbasid period remains. The Sultantepe tablets, discovered nearby by Seton Lloyd and Nuri Gokce in 1951-1952, contained important Assyrian literary texts. The site retains its distinctive beehive-shaped mudbrick houses. Artifacts are in the Archaeological Museum of Sanliurfa, Turkey.

cities.name: Bethlehem
cities.description: Bethlehem, meaning 'House of Bread,' holds a unique place in the story of redemption. Rachel, beloved wife of Jacob, died and was buried on the road to Bethlehem (Genesis 35:19). It was the setting for the book of Ruth, where a Moabite woman's loyalty led her into the lineage of King David. Samuel came to Bethlehem to anoint David, the shepherd boy, as king over Israel (1 Samuel 16:1). The prophet Micah foretold that out of Bethlehem would come a ruler whose origins were 'from of old, from everlasting' (Micah 5:2). Jesus was born in Bethlehem, fulfilling this prophecy (Matthew 2:1, Luke 2:4-7).
cities.scholarly_context: The Church of the Nativity, built by Emperor Constantine in 326 AD over the traditional site of Jesus's birth, is one of the oldest continuously operating churches in the world. Excavations beneath the church by William Harvey (1934) and the Custody of the Holy Land (2010s) have revealed original Constantinian-era mosaic floors and portions of the 4th-century structure. The Bethlehem burial cave discovered in 2016 yielded Iron Age pottery consistent with the Judahite period. Artifacts are housed in the Franciscan Museum in Bethlehem and the Israel Antiquities Authority.

cities.name: Nazareth
cities.description: Nazareth was the hometown of Jesus and the place where the angel Gabriel announced to Mary that she would bear the Son of the Most High (Luke 1:26-27). After the return from Egypt, Joseph and Mary settled there, and Jesus grew up in Nazareth (Luke 2:39, Matthew 2:23). When Jesus read from the scroll of Isaiah in the Nazareth synagogue and declared the prophecy fulfilled, the townspeople attempted to throw Him off a cliff (Luke 4:16-30). Nathanael's famous question, 'Can any good thing come out of Nazareth?' (John 1:46), reflects the town's humble reputation.
cities.scholarly_context: The Church of the Annunciation, rebuilt in 1969 over earlier Byzantine and Crusader structures, sits above caves and domestic installations dating to the 1st century. Excavations by Bellarmino Bagatti (1955-1965) beneath the church uncovered a pre-Byzantine Jewish-Christian synagogue-church, pottery, and agricultural installations from the time of Jesus. A residential structure from the early Roman period, excavated by Yardenna Alexandre (2009), was identified as a typical 1st-century Galilean dwelling. Artifacts are in the Franciscan Museum at the Church of the Annunciation and the Israel Antiquities Authority.

cities.name: Capernaum
cities.description: Capernaum served as the headquarters of Jesus's Galilean ministry and is called 'His own city' (Matthew 9:1). It was here that Jesus called Matthew the tax collector (Matthew 9:9), healed the centurion's servant (Matthew 8:5-13), raised Jairus's daughter, and delivered the Bread of Life discourse in the synagogue (John 6:59). Peter, Andrew, James, and John were all from Capernaum. Despite the mighty works performed there, Jesus pronounced woe upon the city for its unbelief: 'And thou, Capernaum, which art exalted unto heaven, shalt be brought down to hell' (Matthew 11:23).
cities.scholarly_context: The Franciscan Custody of the Holy Land has excavated Capernaum (Tell Hum) since 1905, led by Virgilio Corbo and Stanislao Loffreda (1968-2003). They uncovered a magnificent 4th-century limestone synagogue built over a 1st-century basalt synagogue where Jesus likely taught, and an octagonal Byzantine church built over a house church identified as Peter's house. A Greek Orthodox excavation on the eastern portion of the site revealed additional residential structures. Artifacts remain on-site and in the Franciscan Museum, Jerusalem.

cities.name: Damascus
cities.description: Damascus is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world and features prominently throughout Scripture. Abraham's steward Eliezer was from Damascus (Genesis 15:2), and Abraham pursued the kings who captured Lot as far as Damascus (Genesis 14:15). David placed garrisons in Syria of Damascus after defeating its army (2 Samuel 8:5-6). During the divided kingdom, Damascus was the powerful capital of Aram, often at war with Israel (1 Kings 11:24, 2 Kings 5:12). Isaiah prophesied that Damascus would cease to be a city (Isaiah 17:1). Most significantly, it was on the road to Damascus that Saul of Tarsus encountered the risen Christ and was converted (Acts 9:1-19).
cities.scholarly_context: Damascus's continuous habitation makes extensive excavation difficult. The Great Umayyad Mosque sits on a site that was successively a Roman temple of Jupiter and a Byzantine church. French archaeologists under Jean Sauvaget studied the ancient street grid in the 1930s-1940s, confirming the Roman-era 'Street Called Straight' mentioned in Acts 9:11. Excavations at Tell Ramad on the city's outskirts revealed Neolithic settlement dating to 8000 BC. Artifacts from the Damascus region are housed in the National Museum of Damascus.

cities.name: Tyre
cities.description: Tyre was the greatest of the Phoenician maritime cities, renowned for its wealth, purple dye industry, and seemingly impregnable island fortress. King Hiram of Tyre supplied cedar wood and skilled craftsmen for Solomon's Temple (1 Kings 5:1, 1 Kings 7:13-14). Ezekiel devoted three chapters to prophesying Tyre's destruction in vivid detail, comparing it to a magnificent ship that would sink (Ezekiel 26:1-28:19). Alexander the Great fulfilled Ezekiel's prophecy in 332 BC by building a causeway to the island city using rubble from the mainland ruins, literally scraping the city 'like the top of a rock' (Ezekiel 26:4). Jesus visited the region of Tyre during His ministry (Matthew 15:21).
cities.scholarly_context: Excavations at Tyre (modern Sur, Lebanon) have been conducted by Maurice Chehab (1947-1975) and Patricia Bikai (1973), revealing Phoenician, Roman, and Byzantine layers. The Al-Bass necropolis, excavated by Maria Eugenia Aubet (1997-2012), uncovered the largest Phoenician cremation cemetery ever found, with hundreds of cinerary urns dating to the 9th-7th centuries BC. Extensive Roman ruins including a hippodrome, colonnaded streets, and an aqueduct are visible today. Artifacts are in the National Museum of Beirut and the Louvre.

cities.name: Sidon
cities.description: Sidon was one of the oldest and most important Phoenician cities, named after Canaan's firstborn son (Genesis 10:15). It was renowned for its skilled craftsmen, maritime trade, and purple dye production. Jezebel, the infamous wife of King Ahab who promoted Baal worship in Israel, was the daughter of Ethbaal king of Sidon (1 Kings 16:31). Ezekiel prophesied judgment against Sidon, declaring that pestilence and blood would be in her streets (Ezekiel 28:20-24). Jesus visited the region of Tyre and Sidon where He healed the Syrophoenician woman's daughter (Mark 7:24-31). Paul stopped at Sidon on his voyage to Rome (Acts 27:3).
cities.scholarly_context: Modern Saida in Lebanon occupies the ancient site. Excavations have revealed Phoenician, Persian, and Hellenistic remains including the elaborate Alexander Sarcophagus and the Sarcophagus of the Mourning Women, both found in the royal necropolis at Ayaa by Osman Hamdi Bey in 1887. The Temple of Eshmun, excavated by Maurice Dunand (1963-1975), revealed extensive Phoenician religious architecture and inscriptions. Artifacts are in the Istanbul Archaeological Museums, the National Museum of Beirut, and the Louvre.

cities.name: Samaria
cities.description: Samaria was the capital of the Northern Kingdom of Israel, built by King Omri on a hill he purchased for two talents of silver (1 Kings 16:24). It became a center of Baal worship under Ahab and Jezebel, who built a temple to Baal there (1 Kings 16:32). The city endured dramatic sieges by the Arameans, during which the famine was so severe that a donkey's head sold for eighty pieces of silver (2 Kings 6:24-25). In 722 BC, the Assyrians conquered Samaria and deported the Israelites, fulfilling the prophets' warnings (2 Kings 17:5-6). In the New Testament, Philip preached the gospel in Samaria, and many believed (Acts 8:5-8).
cities.scholarly_context: George Reisner and Clarence Fisher excavated Samaria (Sebastia) for Harvard University (1908-1910), discovering the impressive Israelite royal palace and the Samaria Ostraca — 63 inscribed potsherds recording oil and wine shipments from the 8th century BC. John Crowfoot and Kathleen Kenyon continued excavations (1931-1935), uncovering the Samaria Ivories — exquisite carved ivory panels that may be from Ahab's 'ivory house' mentioned in 1 Kings 22:39. The ivories are in the Palestine Archaeological Museum (Rockefeller Museum), Jerusalem, and the British Museum.

cities.name: Shechem
cities.description: Shechem was the first place in Canaan where Abraham built an altar after the Most High promised the land to his descendants (Genesis 12:6). Jacob purchased a plot of land there and set up an altar (Genesis 33:18-20), though the city was later the site of the violent revenge by Simeon and Levi after the violation of their sister Dinah (Genesis 34:1-31). Joshua gathered all the tribes at Shechem for his final address, where they renewed their covenant with the Most High (Joshua 24:1-27). Abimelech, Gideon's son, made himself king at Shechem before destroying the city (Judges 9:1-57). Rehoboam went to Shechem to be crowned, where the kingdom was divided (1 Kings 12:1).
cities.scholarly_context: Ernst Sellin and his team excavated Tell Balata (ancient Shechem) from 1913-1934, and G. Ernest Wright led the Drew-McCormick Expedition (1956-1973). They uncovered a massive Middle Bronze Age temple-fortress (the 'Temple of El-Berith' of Judges 9:46), a large courtyard temple, the city's fortification walls with gate system, and evidence of destruction layers matching the biblical narrative. The site yielded important Late Bronze Age cuneiform tablets. Artifacts are in the Rockefeller Museum, Jerusalem, and the Jordan Archaeological Museum.

cities.name: Hebron
cities.description: Hebron is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities and holds deep significance in the patriarchal narratives. Abraham settled near Hebron by the oaks of Mamre, where the Most High appeared to him and promised him a son (Genesis 13:18, Genesis 18:1-15). Abraham purchased the Cave of Machpelah at Hebron as a burial place for Sarah, and it became the family tomb for Abraham, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, and Leah (Genesis 23:2-19). The spies sent by Moses described Hebron as a city of giants (Numbers 13:22). David reigned as king over Judah at Hebron for seven and a half years before capturing Jerusalem (2 Samuel 2:1-4, 2 Samuel 5:1-5).
cities.scholarly_context: The Cave of Machpelah, beneath the massive Herodian-era enclosure wall (the Haram al-Khalil), has never been fully excavated due to its religious sensitivity, though a brief exploration in 1119 by Crusaders reportedly found human remains. Philip Hammond excavated Tell Rumeida (ancient Hebron) in 1964-1966, uncovering Early Bronze Age walls and Middle Bronze Age fortifications. Avi Ofer's survey (1985) confirmed continuous habitation from the Early Bronze Age. Artifacts are in the Israel Museum and the Rockefeller Museum, Jerusalem.

cities.name: Jericho
cities.description: Jericho was the first city conquered by the Israelites when they entered the Promised Land under Joshua. After the Israelites marched around the city for seven days, the walls fell flat at the blast of the trumpets and the shout of the people (Joshua 6:1-27). Rahab the harlot, who hid the Israelite spies, was spared and became an ancestor of David and Jesus (Joshua 2:1, Matthew 1:5). Elijah and Elisha passed through Jericho before Elijah was taken up to heaven (2 Kings 2:4-5). Jesus healed blind Bartimaeus near Jericho and dined with Zacchaeus the tax collector, who climbed a sycamore tree to see Him (Luke 18:35-19:10).
cities.scholarly_context: Kathleen Kenyon's excavations (1952-1958) at Tell es-Sultan revealed Jericho as one of the oldest settlements in the world, with a Neolithic tower dating to approximately 8000 BC. John Garstang previously excavated the site (1930-1936), identifying collapsed walls he attributed to Joshua's conquest, though Kenyon's re-dating sparked ongoing scholarly debate. Bryant Wood's reanalysis (1990) argued the destruction evidence does fit a late 15th-century BC date consistent with the biblical chronology. Artifacts are in the Rockefeller Museum, Jerusalem, the British Museum, and the Ashmolean Museum, Oxford.

cities.name: Bethel
cities.description: Bethel, meaning 'House of God,' was one of the most significant worship sites in Israelite history. Abraham built an altar there when he first entered Canaan (Genesis 12:8). Jacob had his famous dream of a ladder reaching to heaven at Bethel, with angels ascending and descending, and the Most High confirmed the covenant with him (Genesis 28:10-22). He returned to Bethel and built an altar after his reconciliation with Esau (Genesis 35:1-15). Tragically, Jeroboam set up one of his two golden calves at Bethel, making it a center of idolatrous worship (1 Kings 12:28-33). The prophet Amos was expelled from Bethel's sanctuary (Amos 7:13).
cities.scholarly_context: William Foxwell Albright excavated Beitin (identified as ancient Bethel) in 1927 and 1934, with continued work by James Kelso (1954-1960). They found evidence of continuous settlement from the Early Bronze Age through the Byzantine period, with a significant destruction layer from the late 13th century BC. However, the identification of Beitin with Bethel has been challenged by David Livingston, who proposed el-Bireh as an alternative. No temple or cult installations matching the biblical sanctuary have been conclusively identified. Artifacts are in the Pittsburgh Theological Seminary and the Albright Institute, Jerusalem.

cities.name: Beersheba
cities.description: Beersheba marked the southern boundary of Israelite territory, giving rise to the phrase 'from Dan to Beersheba' to describe the full extent of the land (Judges 20:1). Abraham made a covenant with Abimelech at Beersheba and planted a tamarisk tree, calling on the name of the Most High (Genesis 21:31-33). Isaac built an altar there after the Most High appeared to him and renewed the covenant (Genesis 26:23-33). Jacob offered sacrifices at Beersheba before going down to Egypt to see Joseph (Genesis 46:1). The prophet Elijah fled through Beersheba on his way to Mount Horeb after Jezebel threatened his life (1 Kings 19:3).
cities.scholarly_context: Yohanan Aharoni excavated Tell es-Seba (1969-1976), uncovering a well-planned Iron Age Israelite city with a remarkable water system, a governor's palace, storehouses, and a four-horned altar dismantled and reused as building stones — possibly evidence of Hezekiah's cultic reforms. The site has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2005 as part of the 'Biblical Tels.' Ze'ev Herzog continued excavations and published the final reports. Artifacts, including the reconstructed horned altar, are in the Israel Museum, Jerusalem.

cities.name: Gaza
cities.description: Gaza was the southernmost of the five Philistine city-states and a major hub on the ancient trade route between Egypt and Mesopotamia. The Most High set Gaza as a boundary of the Canaanite territory (Genesis 10:19). Samson's most dramatic exploits occurred at Gaza: he carried away the city gates on his shoulders and later, blinded and chained, pulled down the temple of Dagon upon the Philistine lords, killing more in his death than in his life (Judges 16:1-3, 16:21-30). Amos prophesied against Gaza for slave trading with Edom (Amos 1:6-7). Philip the evangelist preached on the road to Gaza, where he baptized the Ethiopian eunuch (Acts 8:26).
cities.scholarly_context: Ancient Gaza lies beneath the modern city, making large-scale excavation extremely difficult. Limited excavations by Flinders Petrie at Tell el-Ajjul (1930-1934), initially identified as ancient Gaza, uncovered rich Late Bronze Age tombs with gold jewelry and Egyptianizing artifacts, though the site is now thought to be a separate settlement. More recent work at Tell es-Sakan by Pierre de Miroschedji (1998-2001) revealed an Egyptian colonial settlement. A large Roman-era synagogue with a mosaic floor depicting King David as Orpheus was found in 1966. Artifacts are in the British Museum and the Rockefeller Museum.

cities.name: Ashdod
cities.description: Ashdod was one of the five principal cities of the Philistines and the location of a major temple of Dagon. When the Philistines captured the Ark of the Covenant after the battle of Ebenezer, they brought it to the temple of Dagon in Ashdod. The next morning the idol of Dagon was found fallen face-down before the Ark, and the people of Ashdod were struck with tumors (1 Samuel 5:1-7). The Anakim (giants) remained in Ashdod after Joshua's campaigns (Joshua 11:22). King Uzziah of Judah broke down the walls of Ashdod (2 Chronicles 26:6). In Nehemiah's time, Israelites who had married women of Ashdod had children who could not speak Hebrew (Nehemiah 13:23-24).
cities.scholarly_context: Moshe Dothan excavated Tell Ashdod (1962-1972) for the Israel Department of Antiquities, uncovering over twenty strata of occupation from the Late Bronze Age through the Byzantine period. He found Philistine bichrome pottery, a famous seated figurine known as 'Ashdoda,' and evidence of the Philistine temple. David Noel Freedman co-directed the later seasons. The site yielded the earliest known Philistine inscription. Artifacts, including the iconic Ashdoda figurine, are in the Israel Museum, Jerusalem and the Corinne Mamane Museum of Philistine Culture in Ashdod.

cities.name: Petra / Sela
cities.description: Petra, known as Sela ('the Rock') in Scripture, was the stronghold capital of Edom, carved into rose-red sandstone cliffs. King Amaziah of Judah captured Sela and renamed it Joktheel (2 Kings 14:7). Isaiah referenced Sela in an oracle against Moab (Isaiah 16:1), and the prophet also described those dwelling in the rock singing praise (Isaiah 42:11). Obadiah's prophecy against Edom directly addresses the pride of those dwelling 'in the clefts of the rock' whose habitation was high: 'though thou exalt thyself as the eagle, and though thou set thy nest among the stars, thence will I bring thee down' (Obadiah 1:3-4).
cities.scholarly_context: Petra became the capital of the Nabataean Kingdom from the 4th century BC to 106 AD. The site was rediscovered for the Western world by Johann Ludwig Burckhardt in 1812. Major excavations have been conducted by Philip Hammond (1961-1990s), the Brown University expedition under Martha Sharp Joukowsky (1993-2010) who excavated the Great Temple, and the American Center of Oriental Research. The Treasury (Al-Khazneh), the Monastery (Ad-Deir), and an elaborate water management system are major features. Artifacts are in the Jordan Archaeological Museum and the Petra Museum.

cities.name: Rome
cities.description: Rome, the capital of the empire that ruled the world during Jesus's birth and the early church, features prominently in the New Testament. Emperor Claudius expelled the Israelites from Rome, which brought Aquila and Priscilla to Corinth where they met Paul (Acts 18:2). Paul longed to visit Rome (Romans 1:11-13) and eventually arrived there as a prisoner to appeal to Caesar (Acts 28:14-16). He wrote his epistle to the Romans, one of the most theologically significant letters in Scripture, to the believers in Rome (Romans 1:7). Paul spent two years in Rome under house arrest, preaching the kingdom of God to all who came to him (Acts 28:30-31). Timothy was with Paul during his second Roman imprisonment (2 Timothy 1:17).
cities.scholarly_context: The Mamertine Prison (Carcer Tullianum), traditionally where Peter and Paul were imprisoned, has been partially excavated. The Catacombs of Rome, particularly those of San Callisto and Priscilla, contain some of the earliest Christian art and burial inscriptions dating to the 2nd-3rd centuries. The Arch of Titus (81 AD) in the Roman Forum depicts the spoils of Jerusalem's Temple being carried in triumph, including the menorah. The Vatican Museums, the Capitoline Museums, and the Museo Nazionale Romano house vast collections of Roman-era artifacts.

cities.name: Athens
cities.description: Athens was the intellectual capital of the ancient world, renowned for its philosophers, and the site of one of Paul's most remarkable sermons. When Paul arrived in Athens on his second missionary journey, his spirit was stirred by the city's idolatry (Acts 17:16). He debated with Epicurean and Stoic philosophers and was brought to the Areopagus (Mars Hill), where he delivered his famous address on the 'Unknown God,' declaring that the Most High does not dwell in temples made with hands (Acts 17:22-31). Some believed, including Dionysius the Areopagite and a woman named Damaris (Acts 17:34). Paul wrote to the Thessalonians that he was willing to be left in Athens alone so Timothy could strengthen them (1 Thessalonians 3:1).
cities.scholarly_context: The Agora of Athens, excavated by the American School of Classical Studies since 1931, has revealed the marketplace where Paul likely debated philosophers. The Areopagus (Mars Hill), a prominent rocky outcrop below the Acropolis, preserves a bronze plaque with Paul's sermon inscribed in Greek. Inscriptions to 'unknown gods' corroborating Acts 17:23 have been found at Pergamum and elsewhere. The Stoa of Attalos has been reconstructed as the Agora Museum. Artifacts are in the National Archaeological Museum of Athens and the Agora Museum.

cities.name: Corinth
cities.description: Corinth was a major commercial hub and one of the most important cities for Paul's missionary work. Paul spent eighteen months there on his second journey, founding the church and working as a tentmaker with Aquila and Priscilla (Acts 18:1-18). When the city's leaders brought Paul before the proconsul Gallio, Gallio dismissed the charges, establishing an important legal precedent for the early church (Acts 18:12-17). Paul wrote two extensive letters to the Corinthian church, addressing divisions, immorality, spiritual gifts, and the resurrection. His famous chapter on love (1 Corinthians 13) and the earliest account of the Last Supper (1 Corinthians 11:23-26) were written to Corinth.
cities.scholarly_context: The American School of Classical Studies has excavated Ancient Corinth since 1896. The Bema (judgment seat) where Paul stood before Gallio (Acts 18:12) has been identified in the Roman forum. The Gallio inscription, discovered at Delphi in 1905, dates Gallio's proconsulship to 51-52 AD, providing one of the most important fixed dates in Pauline chronology. The Erastus inscription, found near the theater, may reference the city treasurer mentioned in Romans 16:23. Artifacts are in the Archaeological Museum of Ancient Corinth.

cities.name: Ephesus
cities.description: Ephesus was one of the greatest cities of the Roman province of Asia and a crucial center for early Christianity. Paul spent over two years there on his third missionary journey, performing extraordinary miracles and teaching daily in the school of Tyrannus (Acts 19:1-10). His preaching caused a riot led by silversmiths who made shrines of the goddess Artemis, fearing loss of their trade (Acts 19:23-41). Paul wrote his letter to the Ephesians about the mystery of the church as the body of Christ. Ephesus was also the first of the seven churches addressed in Revelation, warned that they had left their first love (Revelation 2:1-7). Paul left Timothy at Ephesus to confront false teachers (1 Timothy 1:3).
cities.scholarly_context: The Austrian Archaeological Institute has excavated Ephesus since 1895. Major discoveries include the Library of Celsus, the Great Theater (seating 25,000, the site of the silversmith riot in Acts 19), terrace houses with well-preserved frescoes and mosaics, and the Temple of Artemis — one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, identified by John Turtle Wood in 1869. An inscription mentioning 'silversmiths' confirms the trade described in Acts 19. The Ephesus Museum in Selcuk houses key artifacts, with additional pieces in the Kunsthistorisches Museum, Vienna.

cities.name: Antioch (on the Orontes)
cities.description: Antioch on the Orontes was the third-largest city in the Roman Empire and became the first great center of Gentile Christianity. Believers who fled Jerusalem after Stephen's martyrdom brought the gospel to Antioch, where 'the disciples were called Christians first' (Acts 11:19-26). The church at Antioch sent Barnabas and Paul on the first missionary journey after the Holy Spirit said, 'Separate me Barnabas and Saul for the work whereunto I have called them' (Acts 13:1-3). Paul and Barnabas returned to Antioch to report all that the Most High had done (Acts 14:26-28). It was at Antioch that Paul confronted Peter for withdrawing from Gentile fellowship (Galatians 2:11).
cities.scholarly_context: Princeton University led excavations at Antioch (modern Antakya, Turkey) from 1932-1939, uncovering spectacular Roman and Byzantine mosaics, including the famous Judgment of Paris mosaic. The mosaics are among the finest surviving examples of Roman floor art. Glanville Downey published the definitive ancient history of the city. The Chalice of Antioch, a silver cup found in 1910, was once claimed to be the Holy Grail but is now dated to the 6th century. Mosaics are in the Hatay Archaeology Museum, Antakya, with other pieces in the Worcester Art Museum, the Louvre, and the Princeton University Art Museum.

cities.name: Persepolis
cities.description: Persepolis was the magnificent ceremonial capital of the Persian Empire, built by Darius I and expanded by Xerxes I (the biblical Ahasuerus). While Scripture does not name Persepolis directly, the Persian kings who ruled there profoundly shaped Israelite history. It was likely at Persepolis or Susa that the events of Esther took place, where Ahasuerus held his great feast and Esther risked her life to save her people (Esther 1:1-3). Daniel's vision of the ram and the goat occurred during Persian rule (Daniel 8:2). Alexander the Great burned Persepolis in 330 BC, ending the empire that had allowed the Israelites to return from Babylonian exile.
cities.scholarly_context: Ernst Herzfeld began excavations at Persepolis in 1931 for the Oriental Institute of the University of Chicago, followed by Erich Schmidt (1935-1939). They uncovered the Apadana (audience hall) with its famous reliefs showing tribute-bearers from 23 nations, the Gate of All Nations, the Throne Hall, and the Treasury. The Persepolis Fortification Tablets, over 30,000 clay tablets in Elamite cuneiform, illuminate the administrative workings of Darius's empire. Artifacts are in the National Museum of Iran in Tehran, the Oriental Institute in Chicago, and the site museum.

cities.name: Susa
cities.description: Susa (biblical Shushan) was one of the most important capitals of the Persian Empire and the primary setting for two biblical books. It was at Susa that Nehemiah served as cupbearer to King Artaxerxes and received the devastating news that Jerusalem's walls lay in ruins, prompting his mission to rebuild them (Nehemiah 1:1). The entire book of Esther takes place in the palace at Susa, where Queen Esther risked her life before King Ahasuerus to prevent the annihilation of her people (Esther 1:2, Esther 3:15). Daniel also had his vision of the ram and the goat while at Susa (Daniel 8:2).
cities.scholarly_context: French archaeologists have excavated Susa (modern Shush, Iran) since 1884 when Marcel Dieulafoy began work there, uncovering the glazed brick friezes of the Palace of Darius including the famous Archers' Frieze. Jacques de Morgan (1897-1912) found the Stele of Hammurabi and an Elamite ziggurat. Roman Ghirshman continued excavations from 1946-1967. Jean Perrot directed the French Archaeological Mission (1968-1979), excavating the Acropolis, Apadana, and Royal City. Artifacts are in the Louvre, the National Museum of Iran, and the Haft Tappeh Museum.

cities.name: Megiddo
cities.description: Megiddo commanded the strategic Jezreel Valley pass and witnessed more battles than perhaps any other location in the ancient world. It was one of the Canaanite royal cities defeated by Joshua (Joshua 12:21), and Deborah and Barak fought Sisera's army by the waters of Megiddo (Judges 5:19). Solomon fortified Megiddo as one of his chariot cities (1 Kings 9:15). King Josiah, the last righteous king of Judah, was tragically killed at Megiddo when he confronted Pharaoh Necho II of Egypt (2 Kings 23:29-30). In Revelation, the final battle of Armageddon (Hebrew Har-Megiddo, 'Mountain of Megiddo') derives its name from this ancient site (Revelation 16:16).
cities.scholarly_context: Megiddo has been extensively excavated, beginning with Gottlieb Schumacher (1903-1905) and followed by the University of Chicago's Oriental Institute under Clarence Fisher, P.L.O. Guy, and Gordon Loud (1925-1939). Yigael Yadin conducted excavations in the 1960s-1970s, identifying Solomonic gates. Israel Finkelstein and David Ussishkin led the renewed Tel Aviv University expedition (1994-2014), which re-dated several strata. The site was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2005. Artifacts are in the Israel Museum, the Rockefeller Museum, and the Oriental Institute, Chicago.

cities.name: Lachish
cities.description: Lachish was the second most important city of the Kingdom of Judah after Jerusalem and a key military fortress guarding the southwestern approach. Joshua conquered Lachish during the Israelite invasion of Canaan (Joshua 10:3-35). King Rehoboam fortified it as part of Judah's defenses (2 Chronicles 11:9). Sennacherib of Assyria besieged and captured Lachish in 701 BC, an event he commemorated with dramatic wall reliefs in his palace at Nineveh (2 Kings 18:13-14). Lachish was among the last cities to fall to Nebuchadnezzar before the final destruction of Jerusalem, as Jeremiah records: 'the army of the king of Babylon fought against Jerusalem, and against all the cities of Judah that were left, against Lachish, and against Azekah' (Jeremiah 34:7).
cities.scholarly_context: James Leslie Starkey excavated Tell ed-Duweir (ancient Lachish) from 1932-1938, discovering the Lachish Letters — 21 ostraca written in Hebrew describing the desperate final days before Babylonian conquest, including one that says 'we cannot see the signals of Lachish.' David Ussishkin led renewed excavations (1973-1994), meticulously documenting the Assyrian siege ramp — the oldest known siege ramp in the world. Sennacherib's relief panels depicting the siege of Lachish are in the British Museum. Other artifacts are in the Israel Museum and the British Museum.

cities.name: Hazor
cities.description: Hazor was the largest and most important Canaanite city in the land, described as 'the head of all those kingdoms' (Joshua 11:10). Its king Jabin led a massive coalition of Canaanite armies against Joshua, but the Israelites defeated them and burned Hazor — the only city Joshua specifically burned (Joshua 11:1-13). A later King Jabin of Hazor oppressed Israel during the period of the Judges until Deborah and Barak defeated his general Sisera (Judges 4:2). Solomon rebuilt and fortified Hazor as one of his three great military cities alongside Megiddo and Gezer (1 Kings 9:15). Tiglath-Pileser III of Assyria conquered Hazor in 732 BC (2 Kings 15:29).
cities.scholarly_context: Yigael Yadin excavated Tel Hazor (1955-1958, 1968-1969) for the Hebrew University of Jerusalem, uncovering a massive Canaanite city with temples, palaces, and a destruction layer he attributed to Joshua. Amnon Ben-Tor has directed the renewed excavations since 1990, confirming a violent destruction in the 13th century BC with evidence of deliberate burning and decapitated statues. The discovery of a cuneiform tablet mentioning Hazor and correspondence with Mari and Egypt confirm its status as the dominant Canaanite city. The site is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Artifacts are in the Israel Museum, Jerusalem.

cities.name: Dan
cities.description: Dan marked the northern extremity of Israelite territory, forming the proverbial phrase 'from Dan to Beersheba.' Originally called Laish, it was conquered by the tribe of Dan who were seeking an inheritance (Judges 18:27-29). Abraham pursued the kings who captured Lot as far as Dan (Genesis 14:14). When Jeroboam divided the kingdom, he set up one of his two golden calves at Dan, establishing an alternative worship site to Jerusalem and leading Israel into sin: 'this thing became a sin' (1 Kings 12:29-30). The cult center persisted through the Northern Kingdom's history until the Assyrian conquest (2 Kings 10:29). Amos condemned those who swore by the cult of Dan (Amos 8:14).
cities.scholarly_context: Avraham Biran excavated Tel Dan (1966-1999) for Hebrew Union College, making several landmark discoveries. The Tel Dan Stele, found in 1993-1994, contains the phrase 'House of David' — the first extra-biblical reference to King David, and dates to the 9th century BC. Biran also uncovered a remarkable high place with a metal-working platform identified as the bamah where Jeroboam's golden calf was placed, a well-preserved Israelite gate complex, and a Canaanite mud-brick gate arch from the Middle Bronze Age, the oldest known arch in the world. Artifacts are in the Israel Museum and the Skirball Museum, Jerusalem.

cities.name: Dothan
cities.description: Dothan was the site of two dramatic biblical events separated by centuries. Joseph's brothers were pasturing their flocks near Dothan when they saw him coming and conspired to kill him. Instead, they cast him into a pit and sold him to Ishmaelite traders heading to Egypt, setting in motion the divine plan to preserve the family of Israel during the famine (Genesis 37:17). Centuries later, when the king of Aram sent horses, chariots, and a great army to Dothan to capture the prophet Elisha, the prophet prayed that his servant's eyes would be opened. The servant saw the hills full of horses and chariots of fire surrounding Elisha, and the Aramean army was struck with blindness (2 Kings 6:13-23).
cities.scholarly_context: Joseph Free excavated Tell Dothan (1953-1964) for Wheaton College, uncovering occupation levels from the Early Bronze Age through the Hellenistic period. The excavations revealed a large Middle Bronze Age tomb with over 3,000 objects, a well-preserved Iron Age domestic quarter, and a Late Bronze Age destruction layer. Robert Cooley continued analysis and publication of the findings. The site's location along the major trade route from the Jezreel Valley confirms its identification with the caravan route described in the Joseph narrative. Artifacts are at the Wheaton College Archaeology Museum, Illinois.

cities.name: Shiloh
cities.description: Shiloh served as the first permanent worship center for the Israelites in the Promised Land, where the Tabernacle was set up after the conquest of Canaan (Joshua 18:1). The remaining tribal territories were distributed by lot at Shiloh (Joshua 18:8-10), and the annual festival of the Most High was celebrated there (Judges 21:19-21). Hannah prayed at Shiloh for a son and dedicated Samuel to serve there under Eli the priest (1 Samuel 1:3). The Ark of the Covenant was kept at Shiloh until the Philistines captured it after the disastrous battle of Ebenezer (1 Samuel 4:3-4). Jeremiah later warned that Jerusalem would become like Shiloh, which the Most High had destroyed because of Israel's wickedness (Jeremiah 7:12-14).
cities.scholarly_context: Danish expeditions led by Hans Kjaer (1926-1932) first excavated Khirbet Seilun (ancient Shiloh), finding evidence of occupation from the Middle Bronze Age through the Byzantine period. Israel Finkelstein led a major excavation (1981-1984) that revealed Iron Age I remains including storage rooms with collared-rim jars and a destruction layer from the mid-11th century BC, possibly corresponding to the Philistine destruction after the battle of Ebenezer. Scott Stripling's recent excavations (2017-present) have uncovered significant new finds including a possible altar platform. Artifacts are in the Israel Museum and the Danish National Museum, Copenhagen.

cities.name: Gibeon
cities.description: Gibeon was the Hivite city whose inhabitants deceived Joshua into making a peace treaty by pretending to be from a far country (Joshua 9:3-27). When five Amorite kings attacked Gibeon for making peace with Israel, Joshua came to their defense, and the Most High cast down great hailstones and made the sun stand still until the battle was won (Joshua 10:1-14). The pool of Gibeon was the site of a brutal contest between the men of David and the men of Saul (2 Samuel 2:12-17). Most significantly, the Tabernacle was set at the high place of Gibeon during David's reign, and it was there that Solomon offered a thousand burnt offerings and received wisdom from the Most High in a dream (1 Kings 3:4-15).
cities.scholarly_context: James Pritchard excavated el-Jib (ancient Gibeon) from 1956-1962 for the University of Pennsylvania Museum, making spectacular discoveries. He found the 'pool of Gibeon' (a massive cylindrical water shaft, 11 meters in diameter and 10 meters deep) exactly matching 2 Samuel 2:13. He also uncovered an extensive wine-production facility with 63 rock-cut cellars and jar handles stamped with the name 'Gibeon' in Hebrew. An elaborate water tunnel leading to a spring outside the city walls was also found. Artifacts are in the Penn Museum, Philadelphia, and the Rockefeller Museum, Jerusalem.

cities.name: Bethany
cities.description: Bethany was a small village on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives, less than two miles from Jerusalem, and the home of some of Jesus's closest friends. It was here that Lazarus lived with his sisters Mary and Martha, and where Jesus performed His most dramatic miracle — raising Lazarus from the dead after four days in the tomb (John 11:1-44). Mary anointed Jesus's feet with costly spikenard at Bethany, filling the house with fragrance (John 12:1-8). Jesus lodged at Bethany during His final week before the crucifixion (Matthew 21:17). It was near Bethany that Jesus ascended to heaven after His resurrection, lifting His hands and blessing His disciples (Luke 24:50).
cities.scholarly_context: The Church of St. Lazarus in the modern village of al-Eizariya (an Arabic form of 'Lazarion') was built in 1954 over the remains of earlier Byzantine and Crusader churches. Sylvester Saller excavated the site in 1949-1953 for the Franciscan Custody of the Holy Land, uncovering a 4th-century church, rock-cut tombs, and evidence of 1st-century habitation. The traditional Tomb of Lazarus, a rock-cut chamber with a 24-step entrance added by Muslims in the 16th century, can still be visited. Artifacts are in the Franciscan Museum of the Flagellation, Jerusalem.

cities.name: Caesarea Maritima
cities.description: Caesarea Maritima was the Roman administrative capital of Judea and a key location in the book of Acts. Herod the Great built this magnificent port city and named it in honor of Caesar Augustus. Philip the evangelist settled there (Acts 8:40), and it was at Caesarea that the centurion Cornelius received the Holy Spirit, marking the beginning of the gospel going to the Gentiles (Acts 10:1-48). King Herod Agrippa I was struck down by an angel at Caesarea because he accepted worship as a god (Acts 12:19-23). Paul was imprisoned there for two years and made his defense before Felix, Festus, and King Agrippa before his voyage to Rome (Acts 23:23-33, Acts 25:1-12).
cities.scholarly_context: Major excavations have been conducted at Caesarea by Antonio Frova (1959-1963), who discovered the Pilate Stone — a limestone inscription bearing the name of Pontius Pilate as prefect of Judea, the only archaeological evidence of Pilate found to date. The Joint Expedition to Caesarea Maritima (JECM) under Robert Bull (1971-1982) and Kenneth Holum's combined Caesarea excavation (1989-2000s) uncovered the Herodian harbor using underwater archaeology, a Roman theater, a hippodrome, and a Crusader-era fortified city. Artifacts are in the Israel Museum, the Rockefeller Museum, and the Sdot Yam Museum at Caesarea.

cities.name: Caesarea Philippi
cities.description: Caesarea Philippi, located at the base of Mount Hermon near the headwaters of the Jordan River, was the site of Peter's great confession of faith. It was here that Jesus asked His disciples, 'Whom do men say that I the Son of man am?' and Peter answered, 'Thou art the Christ, the Son of the living God' (Matthew 16:13-20). Jesus declared that upon this rock He would build His church and gave Peter the keys of the kingdom of heaven. The location was significant: a pagan shrine with a massive cave dedicated to the Greek god Pan stood nearby, making Peter's declaration all the more dramatic against this backdrop of idolatry.
cities.scholarly_context: The site, known as Banias (from the Greek Paneas), preserves the remains of the Pan sanctuary with its niches carved into the rock face, as well as Herodian-era structures. Zvi Uri Ma'oz conducted excavations (1988-1993) uncovering the sacred precinct of Pan, and Andrea Berlin led renewed excavations (1998-present) revealing a palatial complex from the time of Herod Philip. The cave shrine and its carved niches with dedicatory inscriptions can still be seen. The site is now the Banias Nature Reserve. Artifacts are in the Israel Museum and the Golan Archaeological Museum.

cities.name: Joppa
cities.description: Joppa (modern Jaffa) was the main seaport of ancient Israel and the departure point for two of Scripture's most memorable journeys. It was through the port of Joppa that cedar logs from Lebanon were floated for the construction of Solomon's Temple (2 Chronicles 2:16). Jonah fled from the presence of the Most High by boarding a ship at Joppa bound for Tarshish, rather than obey the command to preach to Nineveh (Jonah 1:3). In the New Testament, Peter raised the disciple Tabitha (Dorcas) from the dead at Joppa (Acts 9:36-43). While staying at the house of Simon the tanner in Joppa, Peter received his vision of the great sheet descending from heaven, preparing him to bring the gospel to the Gentile Cornelius (Acts 10:5-8, Acts 11:5).
cities.scholarly_context: The ancient tell of Joppa (Tell Yafo) has been partially excavated amid the modern city of Jaffa (part of Tel Aviv). Jacob Kaplan conducted excavations (1955-1974) uncovering Egyptian, Late Bronze Age, and Iron Age remains, including a 13th-century BC Egyptian gate bearing the name of Ramesses II. Aaron Burke and Martin Peilstocker directed the Jaffa Cultural Heritage Project (2007-2013), revealing continuous occupation from the Middle Bronze Age. A 3rd-century BC inscription naming 'Joppa' was found during harbor excavations. Artifacts are in the Museum of Antiquities of Tel Aviv-Yafo and the Israel Museum.

cities.name: Kir-hareseth (Kerak)
cities.description: Kir-hareseth (also Kir-heres or Kir of Moab) was the fortified capital of the kingdom of Moab, perched on a rocky height in the Transjordan highlands. When the combined armies of Israel, Judah, and Edom besieged Moab, King Mesha retreated to Kir-hareseth as his last stronghold. In desperation, he sacrificed his firstborn son on the wall, and the siege was lifted (2 Kings 3:25-27). Isaiah's oracle against Moab declares that the foundations of Kir-hareseth shall be mourned (Isaiah 16:7) and that his own heart 'shall sound like an harp for Kir-heres' (Isaiah 16:11). Isaiah also prophesied that Moab's entire land would be laid waste, with Kir at its heart (Isaiah 15:1).
cities.scholarly_context: The site is identified with modern Kerak, Jordan, dominated by the massive Crusader castle (Crac des Moabites) built by Payen le Bouteiller in 1142. The Crusader fortifications largely obscure earlier remains, making archaeological investigation of the biblical-era city difficult. Crystal Bennett conducted limited excavations in the 1980s at the castle area. The Mesha Stele (Moabite Stone), found at nearby Dhiban in 1868 by Frederick Augustus Klein, describes King Mesha's revolt against Israel from the Moabite perspective and is one of the longest Iron Age inscriptions ever found. It is in the Louvre.

cities.name: Bozrah
cities.description: Bozrah was the ancient capital of Edom, the land of Esau's descendants, and features prominently in prophetic oracles of judgment. Jobab son of Zerah of Bozrah was an early Edomite king (Genesis 36:33). Isaiah's dramatic vision of divine vengeance portrays the Lord coming from Bozrah with garments stained crimson, having trodden the winepress alone (Isaiah 63:1). Isaiah also declared that Bozrah would become a desolation, a reproach, a waste, and a curse (Jeremiah 49:13). Amos prophesied fire upon the palaces of Bozrah because Edom pursued his brother with the sword (Amos 1:12). The city's judgment was tied to Edom's perpetual hostility against Israel.
cities.scholarly_context: Bozrah is identified with Buseirah in southern Jordan, excavated by Crystal-M. Bennett (1971-1980) for the British School of Archaeology in Jerusalem. She uncovered a significant Iron Age administrative center with a large building complex interpreted as a palace-temple on the acropolis, along with domestic quarters and industrial installations. The site shows occupation from the 8th through 6th centuries BC, consistent with its role as the Edomite capital. Piotr Bienkowski published the final excavation reports. Artifacts are in the Jordan Archaeological Museum, Amman.

cities.name: Meroe
cities.description: Meroe was the royal capital of the Kingdom of Cush (biblical Ethiopia), the powerful Nubian realm south of Egypt. Scripture speaks of the wealth and power of Cush in several passages, and Isaiah describes a land 'beyond the rivers of Cush' that sends ambassadors by sea (Isaiah 18:1-2). The most direct biblical connection to Meroe is the Ethiopian eunuch encountered by Philip on the road to Gaza — 'a man of Ethiopia, a eunuch of great authority under Candace queen of the Ethiopians' (Acts 8:27). This official had been to Jerusalem to worship and was reading from the scroll of Isaiah when Philip explained the gospel to him and baptized him.
cities.scholarly_context: John Garstang excavated the Royal City of Meroe (1909-1914), and George Reisner excavated the royal pyramids and cemeteries for Harvard University and the Boston Museum of Fine Arts (1916-1923). Over 200 small steep-sided pyramids in the royal cemeteries of Meroe, Nuri, and El-Kurru represent the largest concentration of pyramids anywhere in the world. The title 'Candace' (Kandake) in Acts 8:27 has been confirmed as the title of Meroitic ruling queens through numerous inscriptions. Artifacts are in the Sudan National Museum in Khartoum, the Museum of Fine Arts in Boston, and the Egyptian Museum in Berlin.

cities.name: Alexandria
cities.description: Alexandria, founded by Alexander the Great in 331 BC, became the intellectual capital of the ancient world and home to the largest Israelite community outside the land of Israel. It was here that the Septuagint (Greek translation of the Hebrew Scriptures) was produced, making the word of the Most High accessible to the Greek-speaking world. Apollos, the eloquent preacher who became a powerful advocate for Christ, was a native of Alexandria (Acts 18:24). Israelites from Alexandria were among those who disputed with Stephen in Jerusalem (Acts 6:9). Paul sailed on Alexandrian grain ships during his voyages (Acts 27:6, Acts 28:11).
cities.scholarly_context: Alexandria's ancient remains lie mostly beneath the modern city and underwater in the harbor. Franck Goddio's underwater excavations (1996-present) in the Eastern Harbor have revealed portions of the ancient royal quarter, including what may be Cleopatra's palace complex. The Pharos lighthouse site has been partially mapped. The Serapeum, excavated by Alan Rowe (1943-1945), yielded a foundation deposit confirming its date. The catacombs of Kom el-Shoqafa blend Egyptian and Greco-Roman funerary styles. Artifacts are in the Greco-Roman Museum of Alexandria, the Bibliotheca Alexandrina Museum, and the Egyptian Museum, Cairo.

cities.name: Ashkelon
cities.description: Ashkelon was one of the five cities of the Philistine pentapolis and a major seaport on the Mediterranean coast. Judah captured Ashkelon early in the conquest period (Judges 1:18), but the Philistines later reclaimed it. Samson killed thirty men of Ashkelon and took their garments to pay his wedding wager (Judges 14:19). Zephaniah declared that Ashkelon would be a desolation (Zephaniah 2:4-7), and Zechariah prophesied that the king would perish from Gaza and Ashkelon would not be inhabited (Zechariah 9:5). David's lament over Saul and Jonathan pleaded, 'Tell it not in Gath, publish it not in the streets of Ashkelon, lest the daughters of the Philistines rejoice' (2 Samuel 1:20).
cities.scholarly_context: The Leon Levy Expedition to Ashkelon, directed by Lawrence Stager for Harvard University (1985-2016) and continued by Daniel Master, is one of the most significant excavations in the southern Levant. Discoveries include a Philistine cemetery (2013-2016) providing the first opportunity to study Philistine DNA, a Canaanite silver calf figurine, a massive Middle Bronze Age rampart, and evidence of destruction by Nebuchadnezzar in 604 BC matching Babylonian records. The Ashkelon National Park displays in-situ ruins. Artifacts are in the Israel Museum, the Rockefeller Museum, and the Harvard Semitic Museum.

cities.name: Gath
cities.description: Gath was the most powerful of the five Philistine cities and the home of Goliath, the giant warrior who defied the armies of Israel until the young David killed him with a sling and a stone (1 Samuel 17:4). When David fled from Saul, he twice sought refuge in Gath with King Achish, once feigning madness to escape (1 Samuel 21:10-15, 1 Samuel 27:1-4). The Ark of the Covenant was sent to Gath during its circuit among the Philistine cities, and its people were struck with tumors (1 Samuel 5:8-9). Hazael king of Aram captured Gath during his campaign against the region (2 Kings 12:17). The city's destruction left it largely forgotten for centuries.
cities.scholarly_context: Aren Maeir has directed excavations at Tell es-Safi (ancient Gath) since 1996 for Bar-Ilan University. The dig has revealed one of the largest cities in the ancient Levant during the Iron Age I-II period. Discoveries include a massive siege trench attributed to Hazael of Aram, Philistine 'Aegean-style' hearths, early Philistine cult objects, and an inscription bearing two Proto-Canaanite names etymologically similar to 'Goliath.' Earthquake destruction evidence matching the earthquake in the days of Uzziah (Amos 1:1) has also been found. Artifacts are in the Bar-Ilan University archaeology lab and the Israel Museum.

cities.name: Ekron
cities.description: Ekron was the northernmost of the five Philistine city-states. When the Philistines returned the captured Ark of the Covenant, it passed through Ekron, where the people cried out in fear of the plagues that accompanied it (1 Samuel 5:10). Ekron's five lords sent the Ark back to Israel with guilt offerings of golden tumors and golden mice (1 Samuel 6:17). King Ahaziah of Israel, after falling through a lattice in his upper chamber, sent messengers to inquire of Baal-zebub ('Lord of the Flies'), the god of Ekron, whether he would recover. The prophet Elijah intercepted the messengers with a message of judgment (2 Kings 1:2-3). Zephaniah prophesied Ekron's uprooting (Zephaniah 2:4).
cities.scholarly_context: Trude Dothan and Seymour Gitin excavated Tel Miqne (ancient Ekron) from 1981-1996 for the Albright Institute and Hebrew University. The most spectacular find was a royal dedicatory inscription from the 7th century BC naming 'Achish son of Padi, ruler of Ekron,' confirming the city's identification and matching Assyrian records. The excavations revealed the largest ancient olive oil production facility ever found, with over 100 olive oil presses. Philistine temples, Aegean-style pottery, and evidence of the city's industrial economy were also uncovered. Artifacts are in the Israel Museum and the Albright Institute.

cities.name: Hattusa
cities.description: Hattusa was the capital of the Hittite Empire, one of the great powers of the ancient Near East. The Hittites (sons of Heth) are first mentioned as descendants of Canaan (Genesis 10:15) and appear throughout the patriarchal narratives. Abraham purchased the Cave of Machpelah from Ephron the Hittite in one of the earliest recorded real estate transactions (Genesis 23:3-20). Esau's Hittite wives were a source of grief to Isaac and Rebekah (Genesis 26:34-35). Uriah the Hittite, one of David's mighty men, was the husband of Bathsheba (2 Samuel 11:3). The Hittites were among the nations the Most High commanded Israel to dispossess from Canaan (Deuteronomy 7:1).
cities.scholarly_context: Hugo Winckler discovered the Hittite royal archives at Bogazkoy (ancient Hattusa) in 1906, containing over 10,000 cuneiform tablets that revealed the lost Hittite civilization. Kurt Bittel led German Archaeological Institute excavations (1931-1977), uncovering the Great Temple, the Lion Gate, the King's Gate, and elaborate fortifications. Jurgen Seeher continued excavations from 1994-present. The treaty between Hattusili III and Ramesses II is the oldest known international peace treaty. Hattusa is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Artifacts are in the Museum of Anatolian Civilizations, Ankara, and the Bogazkoy Museum.

cities.name: Rabbah (Amman)
cities.description: Rabbah (modern Amman, Jordan) was the capital of the Ammonites, the descendants of Lot through his younger daughter. The giant iron bed of King Og of Bashan was reportedly kept in Rabbah, measuring nine cubits long (Deuteronomy 3:11). It was during the siege of Rabbah that David committed his great sin, remaining in Jerusalem while Joab besieged the city. David arranged for Uriah the Hittite to be killed in the fighting at Rabbah's walls (2 Samuel 11:1). After Uriah's death, Joab summoned David to take Rabbah himself (2 Samuel 12:26-29). Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and Amos all prophesied against Rabbah for the Ammonites' cruelty toward Israel (Jeremiah 49:2, Ezekiel 25:5, Amos 1:14).
cities.scholarly_context: Ancient Rabbah lies beneath Amman's Citadel (Jabal al-Qala'a), where excavations have revealed remains from the Bronze Age through the Islamic period. The Amman Citadel Inscription, discovered in 1961, is a 9th-century BC Ammonite text found near the temple area. Italian excavations at the nearby 'Ain Ghazal site revealed spectacular Neolithic lime plaster statues, the oldest known large-scale human figures (c. 7000 BC). The Roman Philadelphia's theater and temple of Hercules are well-preserved. Artifacts are in the Jordan Archaeological Museum on the Citadel and the Jordan Museum in downtown Amman.

cities.name: Sardis
cities.description: Sardis was the ancient capital of the fabulously wealthy kingdom of Lydia and one of the seven churches addressed in the book of Revelation. The risen Christ warned the church at Sardis: 'I know thy works, that thou hast a name that thou livest, and art dead' (Revelation 3:1). The church was called to be watchful and strengthen what remained, for their works were not found complete before God. Christ warned that He would come 'as a thief' if they did not watch — a particularly pointed metaphor for Sardis, which had twice been captured by enemies scaling its supposedly impregnable cliffs when the defenders failed to keep watch (Revelation 3:3).
cities.scholarly_context: The Archaeological Exploration of Sardis, conducted by Harvard and Cornell Universities since 1958 under George M.A. Hanfmann and continued by Crawford Greenewalt Jr. and Nicholas Cahill, has uncovered one of the largest ancient synagogues ever found (over 60 meters long), a massive gymnasium-bath complex, and the Temple of Artemis. The gold refinery discovered at the Pactolus River confirmed the ancient tradition that Croesus invented coinage at Sardis. The Lydian Treasure (returned from the Metropolitan Museum to Turkey in 1993) is in the Usak Museum. Other artifacts are in the Archaeological Museum of Manisa.

cities.name: Ecbatana
cities.description: Ecbatana (biblical Achmetha) was the capital of the Median Empire and later served as a summer residence of the Persian kings. Its biblical importance centers on the decree of Cyrus permitting the Israelites to rebuild the Temple in Jerusalem. When opponents tried to halt the Temple reconstruction, King Darius ordered a search of the archives, and the original decree of Cyrus was found at Ecbatana (Ezra 6:2). This discovery confirmed the Israelites' right to rebuild, and Darius issued his own decree supporting the work and threatening anyone who interfered. Ecbatana thus played a crucial role in the restoration of Israelite worship after the Babylonian exile.
cities.scholarly_context: Ancient Ecbatana lies beneath modern Hamadan, Iran, one of the world's oldest continuously inhabited cities, making extensive excavation challenging. Charles Fossey conducted limited work in 1913, and Iranian archaeologists have excavated portions of the ancient mound (Hegmataneh Hill) since the 1980s, uncovering Median and Achaemenid-period walls and a large columned hall. The famous Gold and Silver Rhyton vessels reportedly from the region are in museum collections. The Ganjnameh inscriptions of Darius and Xerxes are carved into rock nearby. Artifacts from excavations are in the Hegmataneh Museum, Hamadan, and the National Museum of Iran, Tehran.

cities.name: Marib
cities.description: Marib was the capital of the ancient Kingdom of Sheba (Saba), from which the Queen of Sheba traveled to test Solomon with hard questions, bringing an enormous caravan laden with gold, spices, and precious stones (1 Kings 10:1-2). Solomon's wealth and wisdom so impressed her that 'there was no more spirit in her' (1 Kings 10:5). Jesus referenced her visit, calling her 'the queen of the south' who 'came from the uttermost parts of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon' and would rise in judgment against that generation (Matthew 12:42). The kingdom of Sheba was known for its incense trade and agricultural wealth, made possible by the Great Dam of Marib.
cities.scholarly_context: The Great Dam of Marib, originally built around the 8th century BC, was one of the engineering marvels of the ancient world, irrigating over 9,600 hectares. Wendell Phillips led the first American expedition to Marib in 1951-1952, though political instability cut the work short. The German Archaeological Institute (DAI) has conducted extensive excavations since the 1980s under Burkhard Vogt, uncovering the Awwam Temple (the 'Mahram Bilqis' or Temple of the Queen of Sheba), the Bar'an Temple, and extensive South Arabian inscriptions. Artifacts are in the National Museum of Yemen, Sana'a, and the Pergamon Museum, Berlin.

cities.name: Kition (Larnaca)
cities.description: Kition (modern Larnaca, Cyprus) was a major Phoenician colony on Cyprus and is associated with the biblical Kittim, descendants of Javan (Genesis 10:4). The term 'Kittim' came to designate not only Cyprus but also, in prophetic usage, the western maritime powers in general. Ships of Kittim appear in Balaam's oracle prophesying that ships from Kittim would afflict Asshur and Eber (Numbers 24:24). Daniel's prophecy references ships of Kittim opposing the king of the North (Daniel 11:30), understood as referring to Roman intervention. The Dead Sea Scrolls use 'Kittim' as a code name for Rome, showing how the term evolved over centuries.
cities.scholarly_context: Vassos Karageorghis excavated the Phoenician temple complex at Kition (1959-1981) for the Cyprus Department of Antiquities, uncovering a major temple of Astarte built over an earlier Late Bronze Age sanctuary, with evidence of copper smelting and extensive Phoenician occupation from the 9th century BC. The Kition archaeological site in Larnaca displays remains of Cyclopean walls and the temple precinct. A Phoenician inscription found at Kition mentions a 'servant of Hiram,' potentially linking the site to the Tyrian kings. Artifacts are in the Larnaca District Archaeological Museum and the Cyprus Museum, Nicosia.

cities.name: Cyrene
cities.description: Cyrene was a major Greek colonial city in North Africa (modern Libya) with a significant Israelite community. Its most notable biblical connection is Simon of Cyrene, who was compelled to carry the cross of Jesus to Golgotha (Matthew 27:32, Mark 15:21). Cyrenians were present in Jerusalem on the day of Pentecost (Acts 2:10) and were among those who debated with Stephen (Acts 6:9). Believers from Cyrene were instrumental in bringing the gospel to Antioch, preaching to Greeks as well as Israelites (Acts 11:20). Lucius of Cyrene was among the prophets and teachers at the church of Antioch who sent Paul and Barnabas on their first missionary journey (Acts 13:1).
cities.scholarly_context: American and Italian archaeological missions have extensively excavated Cyrene (Shahhat, Libya) since the early 20th century. The site preserves a remarkably complete Greek and Roman city including the Temple of Apollo, the Sanctuary of Demeter, the agora, a large theater, and the Baths of Trajan. A Jewish inscription from the 1st century BC found in the synagogue area confirms the significant Israelite community mentioned in Acts. The 'Stele of Cyrene' documents relations between the city and its colonies. Cyrene is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Artifacts are in the archaeological museums of Cyrene and Tripoli.

cities.name: Ctesiphon
cities.description: Ctesiphon was the capital of the Parthian Empire during the New Testament period, situated on the eastern bank of the Tigris River near ancient Babylon. Parthians are listed first among the nationalities present in Jerusalem at Pentecost: 'Parthians, and Medes, and Elamites' heard the apostles speaking in their own tongues (Acts 2:9). The Parthian Empire served as a counterbalance to Roman power throughout the New Testament era, and its eastern frontier was home to significant Israelite communities descended from those who remained in Mesopotamia after the Babylonian exile. The empire's tolerance of diverse religions allowed these communities to flourish for centuries.
cities.scholarly_context: The Taq Kasra (Arch of Ctesiphon), the largest single-span vault of unreinforced brickwork in the world, still stands at the site. Oscar Reuther excavated for the German expedition (1928-1929), and Antonio Ferrante led an Italian mission (1964-1972), uncovering Parthian and Sassanid-era buildings. The site was largely built over by the Sassanid capital, making Parthian-era levels difficult to access. Nearby Seleucia on the Tigris was excavated by the University of Michigan (1927-1937) and yielded extensive Parthian material. Artifacts are in the Iraq Museum, Baghdad, and the Kelsey Museum of Archaeology, University of Michigan.

cities.name: Heshbon
cities.description: Heshbon was the royal city of Sihon, king of the Amorites, who refused to let the Israelites pass through his territory. Moses recounts how the Israelites defeated Sihon and took his city, giving the land to the tribes of Reuben and Gad (Numbers 21:25-26, Deuteronomy 2:24-30). The poet's taunt of Sihon's fall became proverbial: 'Come to Heshbon, let it be built; let the city of Sihon be established' (Numbers 21:27). The Song of Solomon poetically compares the beloved's eyes to 'the pools of Heshbon' (Song of Solomon 7:4). Isaiah and Jeremiah include Heshbon in their oracles against Moab, prophesying its devastation (Isaiah 15:4, Jeremiah 48:2).
cities.scholarly_context: Siegfried Horn and Lawrence Geraty led the Andrews University Heshbon Expedition (1968-1978) at Tell Hesban, though they found limited Late Bronze Age remains, creating a debate about the site's identification with biblical Heshbon. The excavations revealed extensive Iron Age, Hellenistic, Roman, Byzantine, and Islamic occupation. Oystein LaBianca continued the Madaba Plains Project at the site (1984-present), emphasizing long-term food systems and settlement patterns. Some scholars suggest nearby Tell Jalul as an alternative identification. Artifacts are in the Jordan Archaeological Museum and the Horn Archaeological Museum at Andrews University, Michigan.

cities.name: Byblos (Gebal)
cities.description: Byblos (biblical Gebal) was one of the oldest Phoenician cities and a center of maritime trade, shipbuilding, and the papyrus trade that gave the Greeks their word for 'book' (biblion). The Gebalites (men of Byblos) were skilled stonecutters who helped prepare timber and stones for Solomon's Temple (1 Kings 5:18). Byblos is listed among the lands remaining to be conquered when Joshua divided the territory (Joshua 13:5). Ezekiel's lament over Tyre mentions that 'the ancients of Gebal and the wise men thereof were in thee' as ship caulkers, emphasizing Byblos's famed maritime expertise (Ezekiel 27:9).
cities.scholarly_context: Pierre Montet excavated Byblos (modern Jbeil, Lebanon) from 1921-1924, discovering the royal tombs containing the sarcophagus of King Ahiram with the earliest known Phoenician alphabetic inscription (c. 1000 BC). Maurice Dunand continued excavations for over forty years (1926-1975), uncovering the Temple of Baalat Gebal, the Temple of the Obelisks, and remains spanning from the Neolithic period through the Crusader era. Byblos is widely considered one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Artifacts are in the National Museum of Beirut.

cities.name: Ugarit (Ras Shamra)
cities.description: Ugarit was a major Canaanite city-state on the Syrian coast whose religious texts provide the most detailed picture of the Canaanite religion that the Israelites were commanded to reject. While Ugarit is not named directly in Scripture, its destruction around 1185 BC fits within the period of Canaanite displacement described in the conquest narratives. The Canaanite religious practices condemned throughout Deuteronomy and the prophets — worship of Baal, Asherah, and El, sacred prostitution, and child sacrifice — are illuminated in vivid detail by Ugaritic literature. The command to destroy Canaanite altars, pillars, and Asherah poles (Deuteronomy 7:1-5) addressed exactly the religious system documented at Ugarit.
cities.scholarly_context: Claude Schaeffer excavated Ras Shamra (ancient Ugarit) from 1929-1970, with work continued by Marguerite Yon and others through the present. The discovery of thousands of cuneiform tablets in a previously unknown alphabetic script revolutionized the study of the Hebrew Bible. Ugaritic texts reveal Canaanite mythology, including the Baal Cycle and the Legend of Keret, providing direct parallels to biblical Hebrew poetry, vocabulary, and religious concepts. The 30-letter Ugaritic alphabet is the earliest known complete alphabet. Artifacts and tablets are in the National Museum of Damascus, the Louvre, and the Aleppo Museum.

cities.name: Arvad (Arwad Island)
cities.description: Arvad (modern Arwad) is a small island off the Syrian coast, the only inhabited island in Syria, and the home of the Arvadites descended from Canaan (Genesis 10:18). Despite its tiny size, Arvad was a significant Phoenician maritime city-state. Ezekiel's great lament over Tyre mentions that 'the inhabitants of Arvad with thine army were upon thy walls round about, and the Gammadim were in thy towers' — Arvad's warriors served as mercenary defenders of Tyre (Ezekiel 27:11). Ezekiel also notes that 'the men of Arvad with thine army were thy mariners,' highlighting their famed seamanship (Ezekiel 27:8).
cities.scholarly_context: Arvad (Arwad Island) is the smallest inhabited island in the Mediterranean and has seen limited archaeological investigation due to continuous habitation. French archaeologist Ernest Renan surveyed the island in 1860-1861 as part of his Mission de Phenicie, documenting Phoenician walls and harbor installations. The island preserves massive Phoenician-era fortification walls of fitted stone blocks without mortar. Arvad is mentioned in Assyrian records of Tiglath-Pileser I and later Assyrian kings as a tribute-paying vassal. Limited finds are in the National Museum of Damascus and the Tartus Museum.

cities.name: Al-Bad (ancient Midian)
cities.description: Al-Bad, identified as the ancient city of Midian, was where Moses fled after killing an Egyptian and spent forty years as a shepherd before the Most High called him at the burning bush. Moses married Zipporah, daughter of Jethro the priest of Midian, and tended his flocks in the wilderness (Exodus 2:15). It was in this region that the Most High appeared to Moses in the burning bush and commissioned him to deliver the Israelites from Egypt: 'I AM THAT I AM' (Exodus 3:1-14). Jethro later visited Moses in the wilderness and advised him to establish a system of judges to help govern the people (Exodus 18:1-27).
cities.scholarly_context: Al-Bad in northwestern Saudi Arabia preserves rock-cut tombs and structures known locally as the 'Caves of Shuaib' (Jethro). Peter Parr conducted a survey of the Midian region for the University of London (1968-1972), identifying pottery and settlement patterns consistent with Late Bronze and Iron Age occupation. The Saudi-German archaeological project has documented the site's rock-cut chambers, which may represent Nabataean-era modifications of earlier structures. The site is near Magna (ancient Midian/Modiana of classical sources). Finds are housed in the National Museum of Saudi Arabia, Riyadh.

cities.name: Al-Ula (ancient Dedan)
cities.description: Al-Ula, identified with ancient Dedan, was a major oasis city and trading center on the incense route in northwestern Arabia. Isaiah's oracle 'upon Arabia' mentions Dedan's traveling companies, caravans that would be forced to lodge in the wilderness (Isaiah 21:13). Ezekiel lists Dedan among the trading partners of Tyre, noting that Dedan traded in 'precious clothes for chariots' (Ezekiel 27:20). Dedan is also mentioned alongside Sheba and Tarshish in Ezekiel's prophecy against Gog, with Dedan's merchants questioning the invader's motives (Ezekiel 38:13). The Dedanites were descendants of Abraham through his wife Keturah (Genesis 25:3).
cities.scholarly_context: The ancient site of Dedan at Al-Ula has been investigated by Saudi and international teams since the 1960s. The nearby site of Hegra (Mada'in Salih), a Nabataean city with over 130 monumental rock-cut tombs, was named Saudi Arabia's first UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2008. French and Saudi archaeologists under Laila Nehme have excavated at Hegra since 2002. The Lihyanite kingdom at Dedan left numerous inscriptions in a distinctive South Semitic script. The Royal Commission for AlUla (RCU) has undertaken extensive new archaeological surveys since 2017. Artifacts are in the National Museum of Saudi Arabia and the Al-Ula Museum.

cities.name: Marisa (Maresha)
cities.description: Marisa (biblical Maresha) was a fortified city of Judah that played a role in several significant biblical events. King Rehoboam fortified Maresha as part of the defensive network of Judah (2 Chronicles 11:8). It was near Maresha that King Asa of Judah faced the enormous army of Zerah the Cushite with one million soldiers and three hundred chariots. Asa cried out to the Most High: 'Help us, O LORD our God; for we rest on thee, and in thy name we go against this multitude.' The Most High struck the Cushites before Asa and Judah, delivering a miraculous victory (2 Chronicles 14:9-10). Eliezer of Maresha prophesied against King Jehoshaphat's alliance with wicked King Ahaziah (2 Chronicles 20:37).
cities.scholarly_context: Amos Kloner and Ian Stern have led extensive excavations at Tell Sandahanna (ancient Maresha/Marisa) since the 1980s, uncovering a remarkable Hellenistic-period city with over 500 underground cave complexes, including columbaria (dovecotes), olive presses, cisterns, and elaborately painted burial caves with Sidonian inscriptions. The site was the Idumean capital during the Hellenistic period before John Hyrcanus conquered it in 112 BC. Maresha-Bet Guvrin is a UNESCO World Heritage Site (2014). Artifacts are in the Israel Museum, the Rockefeller Museum, and the Beit Guvrin National Park visitor center.

cities.name: Calah (Nimrud)
cities.description: Calah (biblical Kalhu, modern Nimrud) was one of the great cities of Assyria, built by Asshur according to Genesis: 'Out of that land went forth Asshur, and builded Nineveh, and the city Rehoboth, and Calah' (Genesis 10:11-12). Calah served as the military capital of the Assyrian Empire during the 9th and 8th centuries BC, the very period when Assyria menaced the Northern Kingdom of Israel. It was from Calah that Shalmaneser III and later Tiglath-Pileser III launched campaigns against Israel and its neighbors. The palace reliefs from Calah depict in graphic detail the warfare and deportation practices that fulfilled the prophetic warnings against Israel.
cities.scholarly_context: Austen Henry Layard began excavating Nimrud (ancient Calah) in 1845, discovering the Northwest Palace of Ashurnasirpal II with its massive lamassu (human-headed winged bull) gateway figures and alabaster wall reliefs. Max Mallowan excavated the site (1949-1963), finding the Nimrud Ivories — thousands of carved ivory plaques that represent the finest examples of ancient Near Eastern art. Iraqi archaeologist Muzahim Mahmoud Hussein discovered the tombs of Assyrian queens (1988-1989) containing spectacular gold jewelry. Some artifacts were damaged by ISIS in 2015. Collections are in the Iraq Museum, Baghdad, the British Museum, and the Metropolitan Museum of Art.

cities.name: Erech (Uruk)
cities.description: Erech (biblical Uruk) was one of the earliest cities in human civilization and part of Nimrod's kingdom: 'the beginning of his kingdom was Babel, and Erech, and Accad, and Calneh, in the land of Shinar' (Genesis 10:10). As one of the first great cities built after the Flood, Erech represents the rise of organized human civilization in Mesopotamia. In Ezra's account of opposition to the Temple rebuilding, the 'men of Erech' are listed among the peoples whom the Assyrians had settled in Samaria, who then wrote to King Artaxerxes opposing the work of the returned exiles (Ezra 4:9). The city thus appears at both the dawn and twilight of the Old Testament narrative.
cities.scholarly_context: William Kennet Loftus first excavated Warka (ancient Uruk) in 1849-1854, and the German Archaeological Institute has conducted continuous excavations since 1912. The site has yielded some of the most important discoveries in human history, including the earliest known writing (proto-cuneiform tablets from c. 3400-3100 BC), the Warka Vase (one of the earliest narrative relief sculptures), and the Mask of Warka (the earliest known naturalistic human sculpture). The White Temple and the Eanna precinct represent the earliest monumental architecture. Artifacts are in the Iraq Museum, Baghdad, the Pergamon Museum, Berlin, and the British Museum.
